name: JonBenet Ramsey
description: The JonBenét Ramsey case, involving the unsolved murder of a six-year-old beauty pageant contestant in Boulder, Colorado, in 1996, has spawned numerous conspiracy theories. These theories often center on the involvement of her parents, John and Patsy Ramsey, or her brother, Burke. Proponents of these theories point to inconsistencies in the Ramseys' statements, the staged nature of the ransom note, and perceived peculiarities in the crime scene. For example, some claim the ransom note was written by Patsy Ramsey based on handwriting analysis. Others suggest Burke, the brother, may have accidentally killed JonBenét and the parents staged the scene to cover it up. Mainstream law enforcement and forensic experts have largely dismissed these theories after extensive investigations and DNA analysis, although the case remains officially unsolved. The Boulder Police Department initially focused on the Ramseys, leading to intense media scrutiny and public speculation. Later DNA evidence found on JonBenét's clothing pointed to an unidentified male, leading to the exoneration of the Ramsey family in 2008. However, the 'intruder theory' is also debated, with some arguing the crime scene was too secure for an outsider to have easily entered and exited. The ongoing public fascination with the case is fueled by the lack of a definitive resolution and the inherent complexities of the evidence.
subtitle: Unsolved Murder and Lingering Suspicions

claims.claim: Patsy Ramsey wrote the ransom note.
claims.evidence: Handwriting analysis, the note's length and unusual phrasing, and its placement within the house.
claims.criticism: Handwriting analysis is subjective, and the note could have been written under duress or by someone attempting to mimic Patsy's writing. Experts disagree on the handwriting analysis.

claims.evidence: Burke's behavior after the murder, his alleged history of aggression, and the pineapple found in his stomach.
claims.criticism: There is no conclusive evidence linking Burke to the murder. His behavior could be attributed to trauma, and the pineapple theory is speculative and lacks definitive proof. He was cleared by investigators.

claims.claim: John Ramsey staged the crime scene.
claims.evidence: Inconsistencies in his statements, his initial reluctance to cooperate fully with the police, and the perceived artificiality of the ransom note.
claims.criticism: John Ramsey's behavior could be attributed to grief and shock. There is no concrete evidence proving he staged the crime scene. He was cleared by investigators.

keyFigures.name: Lawrence Schiller
keyFigures.role: Author of "Perfect Murder, Perfect Town", a detailed account of the case

keyFigures.name: Paula Woodward
keyFigures.role: Journalist who investigated the case and wrote "We Have Your Daughter"

keyFigures.name: James Kolar
keyFigures.role: Former Boulder Police Detective, author of "JonBenét: Inside the Ramsey Investigation"

keyFigures.name: Lou Smit
keyFigures.role: Homicide detective who believed in the intruder theory

timeline.event: JonBenét Ramsey is found murdered in her home in Boulder, Colorado.
Grand jury votes to indict John and Patsy Ramsey on charges of child abuse resulting in death, but the DA declines to prosecute due to lack of evidence.
DNA evidence leads to the exoneration of the Ramsey family.
CBS airs "The Case Of: JonBenét Ramsey", reigniting public interest and speculation.

sources.author: Stephen Singular
sources.work: Presumed Guilty: An Investigation into the JonBenét Ramsey Case, the Media, and the American Justice System
sources.title: Stephen Singular
